← the Atlas

Electricity — identified, and their progress

analysis · evergreen ·JUN 08, 2026

A tracker for kolu's electricities — infrastructure pulled out from beneath the app into its own thing — and where each stands.

Contents

  • Four transports hold the framework up
  • The electricities
  • Next: extract @kolu/solid-xterm
  • Considered — not electricity

What counts as electricity

Löwy’s receptacle — hide the volatility behind a socket you plug into. Three tests at once: ① domain-agnostic — no terminal/canvas/git/xterm leaks into the interface; ② hides a hard volatility — transport, reconnect, GPU-context loss, persistence — not a 3-line ===; ③ graduates — ships as @kolu/* and a different app plugs in. The proof of ③ is real, not aspirational. @kolu/surface is kolu’s own client↔server transport first — and it has since picked up a second consumer in srid/drishti (a fleet process-monitor; browser ↔ Bun ↔ ssh-agent) that vendors it from this repo and declares its own per-host schema with none of kolu’s domain. The second consumer isn’t the point of the extraction — it’s the proof it was real electricity: surface never noticed the domain changed.

How a session mis-identifies electricity — three traps, learned the hard way

The live example: asked to add back/forward to the Code-tab preview, a session proposed extracting a history stack. Wrong three ways, each caught only by re-asking the test above — and the solid-browser plan is the corrected end-to-end.

  • Naming an organ, not the concept. A history stack is a transformer — a working part inside a larger thing. The electricity was the browser (location + history + link-nav + render-host); history is one chamber of it. Test: is the name self-describing standalone? “history” → history of what?; “browser” → obvious. If you must append “…of X” to explain the name, you’ve named a part, not the receptacle.
  • A module in client/src/ is not electricity. Location is structure (Hickey): a generic-looking helper living inside the app is part of the app, however agnostic the code reads. Electricity is its own package, the dependency arrow pointing out — client → @kolu/x, never back. (createSharedRoot sits in client/src marked “publish?” — a leaf awaiting graduation, not a precedent for calling in-app modules electricity.)
  • Agnostic ≠ electricity — apply test ② honestly. A clean, reusable, agnostic primitive that hides only a bounded algorithm (a stack, a debounce, an ===) is a leaf (nonempty/html-escape-tier), not a receptacle. “I found a tidy generic module” is the feeling that precedes the mistake. Ask what hard volatility it hides — transport, GPU-context loss, sandboxing, reconnect — before reaching for @kolu/.

“Decompose based on volatility.” Functional decomposition splits a system by what it does; volatility-based decomposition isolates each area likely to change behind a stable interface — the way a body keeps blood-pressure, pulse, and salinity volatility “encapsulated behind the service called the heart.” An electricity, here, is one such volatility pulled out into its own package. — Juval Löwy, Volatility-Based Decomposition (excerpt from Righting Software).

Four transports hold the framework up

Strip kolu of its domain and four sockets remain. Each is a transport — it moves one kind of thing across the client↔server↔host gap and stays blind to what flows through it. They are why a different app (drishti) can stand on kolu’s spine at all.

Three carry a payload over a live link:

  • State — @kolu/surface. Cell/Collection/Stream/Event over oRPC, reconnect, fine-grained reconcile. Doesn’t know a terminal tile from a process-monitor row.
  • Process — @kolu/pty-host. One PtyHost owns N PTYs and fans VT-derived taps to N consumers; the createPtyHost primitive knows nothing of git, PRs, agents, the file tree, or shell-env prep — it spawns a ready shell/args/env. (The package’s optional servePtyHost serving layer does prepare the shell env, via kolu-pty’s prepareShellInit/cleanEnv/koluIdentityEnv — that’s the kolu-bound half built on top of the agnostic primitive.)
  • Agent identity — anyagent. AgentProvider<Session, Info> answers “which coding agent is in this terminal, and what is it doing?” — the interface is generic over <Session, Info> and carries no claude/codex token; each integration parameterizes it. (The package does still own concrete CLI vocabulary — STABLE_FLAGS, AgentKind, BASENAME_TO_KIND in agent-cli.ts — but as a detection table the receptacle owns, not a token baked into the provider contract.)

The fourth moves the server itself:

  • Host / link — @kolu/surface-nix-host. HostSession — nix copy a closure to a remote host, realise it, and run a surface --stdio link over ssh. It encapsulates a volatility the other three don’t touch: where the server runs and whether the host is reachable and provisioned (connection lifecycle, reconnect, the nix realise). The other transports ride on top of it for the remote case; it is the substrate that turns “client↔server” into “client↔ssh↔remote-server” without any payload noticing.

The tell that these are transports and not features: each names a verb on an opaque payload — carry state, own a pty, resolve an agent, reach a host — and each already has, or is one schema away from, a consumer that shares none of kolu’s domain. surface-nix-host is the most-proven of the four — both in-repo surface/example/* apps and drishti already stand on it (kolu’s own remote backend is still the future RemoteTerminalBackend, R-2; the kolu server does not yet import @kolu/surface-nix-host). The two newest (pty-host, anyagent) were hiding in plain sight: pty-host lived one rung under the terminal subsystem until #1023 pulled it out; anyagent’s AgentProvider contract was unified in #603 and has been quietly load-bearing ever since. Everything else in the table is either a UI-adapter receptacle (xterm, browser, markdown, pwa-install) or a leaf.

A note on names — the scope prefix is the graduation marker. @kolu/* is a published-shape package: npm-shaped, the dependency arrow points out, ready for a foreign consumer. kolu-* is an in-tree workspace package: extracted, but domain-bound or not yet published-shape (kolu-server, kolu-transcript-core). A few packages drop the scope entirely — nonempty, memorable-names, and tellingly anyagent. anyagent shedding even the kolu name is the strongest signal it sits at framework level and not app level: it’s the receptacle for coding agents, a volatility the whole industry shares, not one kolu owns.

The electricities

ElectricityOwns (volatility)PRProgress
@kolu/surfaceThe state transport — live client↔server state (Cell/Collection/Stream/Event over oRPC), reconnect, fine-grained reconcile. #805 done · 2nd consumer: drishti
@kolu/pty-hostThe process transport — one PtyHost owns N PTYs, each a node-pty child paired with an @xterm/headless screen mirror and VT-derived event taps (cwd / title / commandRun from OSC 7 / 0,2 / 633), fanned to N consumers over a bounded broadcast Channel. The createPtyHost primitive owns only the PTY: it knows nothing of git, PRs, agent-detection, the file tree, or any wire protocol — and nothing of shell-environment prep (callers hand it a ready shell / args / env; kolu-pty builds those). The package’s optional servePtyHost serving layer is the kolu-bound half on top: it prepares the shell env via kolu-pty (prepareShellInit / cleanEnv / koluIdentityEnv) before serving ptyHostSurface over the primitive. The domain-agnostic core of the terminal, pulled out from under it. #1023 done ③ proof-pending
anyagentThe agent-identity transport — AgentProvider<Session, Info>: one socket that detects which coding agent occupies a terminal and streams its live state. resolveSession / sessionKey / createWatcher speak only a domain-agnostic AgentTerminalState (foreground PID, cwd, lazy basename, OSC-633 preexec hint) — the generic AgentProvider<Session, Info> interface carries no claude/codex/opencode token; each integration parameterizes it (AgentProvider<SessionFile, ClaudeCodeInfo>), never widens it. (The package surface is not token-free — agent-cli.ts owns the concrete CLI/kind tables; that’s the receptacle owning its detection vocabulary, not the provider contract carrying a domain token.) Three agents plug in today (claude-code / codex / opencode); four more are name-detected (aider / goose / gemini / cursor-agent). A fourth full agent is a new kolu-* provider package that plugs into the unchanged AgentProvider contract — the arrow points strictly out into anyagent — but it still needs central registration (the AgentKindSchema/AgentInfoSchema union in kolu-common, provider registration in server/terminalBackend/providers.ts, and the icon/name tables in client/ui/agentDisplay.ts). #603 done ③ proof-pending · 3 in-tree integrations
@kolu/surface-appThe app shell for surface apps — fresh delivery + server/build identity + the connection/update lifecycle model + desktop-install. #1154 done · 2nd consumer: drishti
@kolu/surface-nix-hostThe host/link transport — HostSession: nix copy a closure to a host, realise, run a surface --stdio link over ssh. Owns where the server runs and whether the host is reachable/provisioned (connection lifecycle, reconnect). The substrate the other transports ride on for the remote case. #984 done · 2nd consumer: drishti + both surface/example/* apps
@kolu/artifact-sdkTwo volatilities under one roof — the agnostic anchoring core (W3C quote re-find; speaks only Range/Document/ShadowRoot) and the sandboxed-iframe parent↔frame bridge (opaque-origin postMessage, now carrying comments + in-frame navigation + back/forward input). The re-eval below proposes splitting the bridge into @kolu/sandbox-bridge — the parent side splits clean; the in-iframe half still shares the anchoring core, so the cut line is the open question (see the row + callout). #922 done split proposed
@kolu/sandbox-bridge (proposed — split from artifact-sdk)The in-frame sandbox transport — the parent-side observers bindArtifactSdk / observeIframeNavigation / observeIframeHistory: talk to code inside an opaque-origin sandboxed iframe over postMessage, where event.source === iframe.contentWindow is the only trust boundary (event.origin is the literal string "null", so origin checks are useless). A transport across a different gap than the four above — parent↔sandboxed-frame, not client↔server↔host. The wire types live in types.ts: iframe→parent is SelectMsg / ReadyMsg / HistoryMsg, parent→iframe is PathMsg / RenderHighlightsMsg — the transport-shaped messages are payload-opaque (path + W3C Locator + rect + pathname + direction), but RenderHighlightsMsg does carry a comments array and the message type strings are all kolu-artifact-sdk:*, so the wire is not yet free of the artifact/comment vocabulary (a real split would rename the protocol and lift the comment-shaped payload out, or leave it as the bridge’s one typed client). bridge.ts:59 calls it a “network-grade boundary” that drops unknown message types for forward-compat. Three interchangeable clients ride the one socket — comments (selection→highlights), in-frame navigation, back/forward input — the “set of clients is not fixed” Löwy uses to justify a receptacle. The clean part of the split is the parent side: client/bridge.ts imports zero anchoring-core functions (only the Locator type). The in-iframe agent (iframe/index.ts) is not free of the core — it imports extractQuote + applyHighlights packages/artifact-sdk/src/iframe/index.ts:23-24, so either the in-iframe half ships with the bridge and depends on a shared anchoring leaf, or the anchoring stays in artifact-sdk and only the parent-side observers graduate. Two in-tree clients today (CommentIframeSurface + BrowseIframeRenderer). #922 to split ③ proof-pending
@kolu/solid-pwa-install”Install this web app,” across the whole engine matrix — Chromium’s deferred beforeinstallprompt captured and replayed on a user gesture vs iOS Share → Add to Home Screen vs macOS-Safari Add to Dock vs Firefox-Android’s hidden menu item vs Firefox-desktop’s nothing — plus the iPadOS-in-desktop-mode UA disguise (an iPad reports a Mac UA; caught only by maxTouchPoints > 1). A consumer that branched on navigator.userAgent itself would carry that matrix forever. Pure detection (detectInstallPlatform / installInstructions, Node-testable) is split from the reactive createPwaInstall() controller; installed-state is deliberately not in this socket (single owner in surface-app’s isInstalled). #1199 done ③ proof-pending
clipboard (ui/clipboard.ts) → candidate @kolu/solid-clipboardCross-platform clipboard — the same kind of engine-fragmentation volatility solid-pwa-install owns. navigator.clipboard exists only in a secure context (https / localhost / 127.0.0.1); plain http:// over a LAN address, hostname, or Tailscale IP makes it undefined with no permission prompt to recover — so writes fall back to a synthetic-selection document.execCommand("copy") (deprecated but caniuse 100/100), while reads (OSC-52 paste) have no safe fallback and return empty. SafeClipboardProvider implements xterm’s IClipboardProvider, so terminal OSC-52 routes through the exact same socket as app copy. Domain-agnostic, one clean module (writeTextToClipboard + SafeClipboardProvider) — but it lives at client/src/ui/clipboard.ts, not in a package, so by this page’s own bar (a module in client/src/ is not electricity; the @kolu/* boundary is the graduation marker) it is a candidate awaiting extraction, the createSharedRoot-tier of “publish?”. 8 in-app consumer files today (9 writeTextToClipboard/SafeClipboardProvider call sites). #953 publish?
@kolu/solid-markdownGFM → HTML as one render socket — marked parse + Shiki highlight + DOMPurify sanitize behind renderMarkdownToRawHtml / sanitizeHtml. The volatility is real and shifting: the markdown spec, Shiki’s bundled grammars, and the sanitization allowlist (a live attack surface every consumer re-tests when it moves). Link/image policy and Obsidian-style wikilink resolution ride along under the same roof — see the ②/③ re-eval note below. #1079 done ②/③ re-eval
kolu-transcript-core + kolu-transcript-htmlTranscript model + rendering. Workspace-scoped (kolu-*), not yet published-shape — extracted, graduation pending. #744 done
@kolu/solid-pierreSolidJS adapters for Pierre tree/diff. #823 done
persistedPrefValidated localStorage (validate-on-read; closed the zoom-NaN + maximize bugs). #1089 done
@kolu/log · @kolu/html-escapeZero-dep leaf types every package can import without dragging the domain tree. #1089 done
dom/ + injectable isMacwalkShadowRoots relocated to a neutral wall; keybind core no longer reads the UA singleton. #1089 done
createSharedRootLazy app-scoped reactive singleton (6 consumers). 100% agnostic SolidJS. #974 done publish?
@kolu/url-shapehasOwnScheme(src) — one DOM-free predicate: does a reference carry its own origin/scheme (#anchor, //host, scheme:) or is it repo-relative? Its own leaf package so two unrelated owners share it without a dependency arrow between them — solid-markdown’s href policy and solid-browser’s relative-path resolver — instead of routing one through the other (which would drag solid-js + DOMPurify into the node-pure resolver). A leaf (bounded predicate); two consumers prove clean leaf boundaries, not transport volatility — sharing a leaf is not the same as hiding a volatility. #1191 done
@kolu/solid-fileviewFileView — the Source⇄Rendered toggle orchestrator: it picks the first renderer whose match(path) claims a file, owns the mode state, and mounts the active appliance. The FileView core has zero hard-coded renderers — it depends on none; the image / iframe / markdown / video appliances ship alongside as separable subpath exports (@kolu/solid-fileview/renderers/*) that the host opts into and injects. A leaf (bounded UI mechanism): the hard volatility (“how each file type renders”) lives in the injected appliances and the app, not in the toggle. The clean, agnostic, pluggable surface is exactly the feeling the three traps warn precedes a mis-ID. #1082 done
createWalSubscriptionRefcounted fs.watch over a SQLite -wal file — N consumers share one watcher per DB path, surviving WAL-checkpoint inode replacement via a parent-dir + direct dual-watch. Lives in kolu-shared (sqlite/); two real consumers (codex + opencode WAL watchers). A leaf awaiting graduation — genuine fs-volatility, but Node-bound and integration-only, the createSharedRoot-tier of “publish?“. #784 done publish?
@kolu/serve-dirFetch-native file serving from an absolute root — streaming byte-range (206/416), content-type, lexical traversal guard; a pure (root, relPath, request) → Response. Its own package, zero workspace deps (node:fs/path/stream + the focused mrmime MIME table) — the dependency arrow points out (kolu-server → @kolu/serve-dir). Agnostic of terminals/git/kolu; the consumer injects the root and composes the artifact-sdk <script> decorator downstream. A 20-agent prior-art survey found no static-serve library fits the serving shape (all pipe to a Node res, breaking the Fetch-Response + decorator composition; none takes a per-request absolute root or does a realpath guard), so the serving is owned here — but the MIME table leans on mrmime (the separable commodity the survey explicitly didn’t cover). That’s load-bearing for decomposition: a complete table means adding a format to kolu’s *_EXTENSIONS classifier needs no edit here (mrmime already types it), so the ext↔MIME shared-volatility the lens debated is dissolved for every mrmime-known format. For the mrmime gap set (.m4v/.ico, and any future classifier entry mrmime doesn’t know) the coupling is instead contained-by-test: a 2-entry OVERRIDES in serve-dir supplies the MIME, and the coverage invariant in iframePreviewRoute.test.ts is load-bearing, not a thin sanity check — delete an OVERRIDES row and the classifier still routes the file to a <video>/<img> appliance while serve-dir answers application/octet-stream, so only that test catches the silent download regression. Path safety is two-stage by volatility: the lexical guard (decode-then-split + path.relative containment) is built in (pure, universal), while the realpath/symlink guard is an injected realpathGuard the consumer supplies (fs-touching, threat-model-specific) — kolu-server wires kolu-git’s assertRealpathUnder, so the package stays agnostic without dropping the security boundary. A leaf (bounded algorithm, not a transport-grade volatility). #1225 done ②/③ proof-pending
@kolu/solid-xterm (createSolidXterm)xterm.js lifecycle — 8-addon orchestration, WebGL single-owner + context-loss recovery, FitAddon resize, owner-correct async dispose across await. #1116 (plan)to build
@kolu/solid-browserThe Code tab is a browser — location/address model, back-forward history, cross-content-type link interception, GitHub-relative resolution, sandboxed-iframe lifecycle. git is an injected resolver; the renderer packages plug in. Plan. #1191 phase 1 primitives shipped · createBrowser+history (P2), ③ proof-pending

anyagent — the agent-identity receptacle (the outside-the-box one)

The non-obvious framework-level electricity, found the way surface was: by asking what would a different app on this spine still need, with kolu’s domain stripped out? The answer here is “tell me which coding agent is running, and what it’s doing” — and the volatility behind it is enormous and industry-wide. The set of agents is not fixed and never has been: the package began as integrations/common, was renamed in #544 , and the contract was unified into one AgentProvider in #603 ; codex and opencode detection landed after ( #658 , #677 ). Today three agents are fully wired (claude-code / codex / opencode) and four more are name-detected (aider / goose / gemini / cursor-agent) — the literal “providers change” volatility Löwy uses to justify a receptacle in the first place.

It passes ① and ② honestly; ③ is still proof-pending. ① Domain-agnostic — AgentTerminalState carries foregroundPid, cwd, a lazy readForegroundBasename(), and an OSC-633 preexec hint, and nothing else as data packages/integrations/anyagent/src/agent-provider.ts:27-46 (the field JSDoc names agents as examples — "opencode", "claude" — but no agent name is part of the type). The generic AgentProvider<Session, Info extends AgentInfoShape> interface packages/integrations/anyagent/src/agent-provider.ts:77-95 carries no claude/codex token — integrations parameterize it (AgentProvider<SessionFile, ClaudeCodeInfo>), they don’t widen it. (Note the package is not token-free: agent-cli.ts owns STABLE_FLAGS / AgentKind = "claude-code" | "codex" | "opencode" / BASENAME_TO_KIND — concrete CLI-detection vocabulary the receptacle deliberately owns, distinct from the agnostic provider contract.) ② Hides a hard volatility — session-identity resolution differs wildly per agent (claude-code reads a foreground PID → session file; codex/opencode match a process name → a directory-scoped lookup over a SQLite-WAL tail), and the state-derivation transport differs too (JSONL fs.watch + SDK buffers vs WAL writes). That is genuine transport-grade volatility, not a bounded ===. ③ Graduates — not yet proven by the note’s own bar. The dependency arrow does point strictly out: kolu-claude-code → anyagent, never back (grep finds zero reverse imports), and three independent integration packages already plug into the unchanged socket — strong internal evidence the contract is load-bearing. But ③ asks for a published @kolu/* shape and a different app plugging in, and anyagent is still private: true with only in-tree kolu consumers (kolu-claude-code / kolu-codex / kolu-opencode, plus kolu-client / kolu-server / kolu-common). The three integrations are the same-repo, same-domain workspace — they prove ② (the volatility is real and the socket absorbs it), not ③ (a foreign consumer on a published package). The graduation proof surface gets from drishti, anyagent is one packaging step away from earning.

The tell it sits at framework level and not app level: it sheds even the kolu name. surface is @kolu/surface; the agent receptacle is just anyagent, because coding-agent identity is a volatility the whole industry carries, not one kolu owns. The only thing keeping it off the @kolu/* published-shape tier is packaging, not soundness.

Re-evaluating @kolu/solid-markdown — render socket, or render + policy + wikilink bundle? (open)

The row above ships one clean volatility — the GFM→HTML pipeline (parse + highlight + sanitize) behind renderMarkdownToRawHtml / sanitizeHtml. But two other concerns ride under the same package roof: the link/image URL policy (url-policy.ts — which schemes/hosts are navigable or loadable) and the wikilink resolver (wikilink.ts — Obsidian-style basename matching with ambiguity reporting). Read the exports as a new consumer (§6.5): is this one socket — “render markdown safely” with policy + wikilink as internal submodules — or a topic-bundle of three markdown-adjacent concerns? The honest smell is that the policy already had to spit out its structural half into the separate @kolu/url-shape leaf so solid-browser could share it without importing this package — evidence the URL concern is not fully owned here. Flagged, not decided; the package works and is agnostic either way. Two in-tree consumers today (kolu-client and @kolu/solid-fileview’s markdown appliance) but no out-of-tree consumer, so ③ is unproven as well.

New this pass — two electricities the tracker was missing

A fresh skeptical sweep (clipboard / notifications / service-worker / multi-tab / sandbox / update / file-transfer) turned up two genuine receptacles the doc hadn’t named, plus one honest non-find:

  • @kolu/sandbox-bridge — the headline. It answers the artifact-sdk re-eval below: “talk to code inside an opaque-origin sandboxed frame” is a transport-grade volatility (identity-only trust boundary, N interchangeable clients), and the parent-side observers are already a clean module inside artifact-sdk. The split’s direction is set; its cut line isn’t free — the parent side carries no anchoring-core dependency, but the in-iframe agent shares extractQuote/applyHighlights and the wire still speaks the kolu-artifact-sdk:* comment vocabulary, so the plan is either “parent-only graduates” or “whole bridge graduates atop a shared anchoring leaf” (see the callout). A transport across the parent↔frame gap, distinct from the four network transports.
  • clipboard (candidate @kolu/solid-clipboard) — the second solid-pwa-install-shaped electricity: cross-engine clipboard fragmentation (secure-context Clipboard API vs execCommand fallback vs OSC-52), hidden behind one clean socket — but still an in-app module at client/src/ui/clipboard.ts, so it is a candidate awaiting graduation out of client/src, not yet a @kolu/* package.
  • Not an electricity (recorded so it isn’t re-proposed): the notification/badge channel (setAppBadge + SW showNotification) is a real, hard cross-engine volatility but it is sprayed across useTerminalAlerts + useActivityAlerts with no single owner — a Layer-0 missing seam, not a receptacle yet. See the considered table.

The other four probes (service-worker → already inside surface-app; multi-tab → not a kolu concern; visibility/wake-lock → leaf-sized visibilitychange rules today, no wakeLock usage at all; drag-drop upload → domain-coupled) came back negative — each recorded in the considered table below.

Re-evaluating @kolu/artifact-sdk — split the bridge into @kolu/sandbox-bridge (direction set; cut line open)

“Anchoring” (the original #922 framing) now under-describes the package. It holds two volatilities: (1) the anchoring core (core/ — W3C quote extract/re-find/highlight; pure and agnostic, speaks only Range/Document/ShadowRoot), and (2) the sandboxed-iframe parent↔frame bridge (client/bridge.ts + iframe/ — opaque-origin postMessage with event.source identity as the only trust boundary). The bridge is the harder volatility, and it has quietly accreted clients: comments (selection → SelectMsg → highlights), then in-frame navigation (observeIframeNavigation), then back/forward input (observeIframeHistory, #1191 ). The lens-debate in #1191 already reframed the package’s stated identity to “the in-iframe sandbox bridge, with comments as one client” — but that was a docs fix, not a decomposition.

Direction set: it is one sound receptacle — a transport-grade volatility with comments / navigation / input as interchangeable clients — and it should split from the anchoring core into @kolu/sandbox-bridge (table row above). The supporting evidence: the transport speaks postMessage + event.source identity, bridge.ts:59 already treats it as a “network-grade boundary” that tolerates unknown message types, and the three clients accreted on the same event.source barrier without depending on each other — the “set of clients is not fixed” signature.

But the cut line is not yet mechanical, and the writeup earlier overstated it. Two honest dependencies remain: (1) the wire is not free of the artifact vocabulary — RenderHighlightsMsg carries a comments array and every message type is a kolu-artifact-sdk:* string packages/artifact-sdk/src/types.ts:61-77, so a real split has to rename the protocol and decide whether the comment payload moves out or stays as the bridge’s first typed client; and (2) only the parent side splits clean — client/bridge.ts imports zero anchoring-core functions (just the Locator type) — while the in-iframe agent imports extractQuote and applyHighlights from ../core packages/artifact-sdk/src/iframe/index.ts:23-24. So the real plan is one of: (a) graduate only the parent-side observers and leave the in-iframe SDK + anchoring in artifact-sdk, or (b) ship the whole bridge (parent + iframe) and factor the shared extractQuote/applyHighlights into a node-pure anchoring leaf both sides import. The smell that flagged it — the back/forward client landing here by physical necessity — was real, but the resolution is “extract the transport,” not “the split is free.” ③ is still proof-pending (in-tree consumers only; no foreign app yet).

Plus external electricity correctly leaned on instead of hand-rolled: Corvu (dialog/drawer/focus-trap/scroll-lock), @solid-primitives/* (resize-observer, media). Origin: a 41-agent Hickey/Lowy audit (16 candidates) → its “ship now” tier landed in #1089 ; a framework-scale re-test + adversarial skeptic surfaced the missed solid-xterm.

Next: extract @kolu/solid-xterm

The one genuine not-yet-built electricity — mis-filed at first as “terminal domain,” but a SolidJS adapter for xterm.js is agnostic (the .agency/lowy.md §6.5 “what library is this?” test). It owns two declared volatility rows — async-init cleanup (the #598/#600 ~900 KB leaks) and canvas/WebGL lifecycle (the #591 zombie-context hunt, #575/#239/#595).

Inline today in one Terminal.tsx onMount (972 lines): new XTerm packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:472, 8× loadAddon packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:489-509, the WebGL single-owner dance packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:222-285, owner-correct async init packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:463-471, fit/resize packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:179 / packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:750, touch-scroll packages/client/src/terminal/Terminal.tsx:549-596.

// one primitive (not a topic-bundle, per §6.5):
createSolidXterm({ container, theme, fontSize, canUseWebgl?, enabledAddons?,
  onData?, onResize?, onLinkActivate?, onCustomKeyEvent?, plugins? })
  -> { terminal, searchAddon, hasWebgl, renderer, dispose }

Owns the agnostic core; kolu bits (link provider, soft-keyboard, diagnostics probes, the PTY terminal.attach stream) stay consumer-injected via callbacks. Dissolves ~240–314 lines of Terminal.tsx. Survived the skeptic (“the domain braid runs too deep”) on the interface — those are orchestration concerns, not xterm-lifecycle concerns. Graduates the way surface did: any SolidJS app embedding xterm can plug in.

Considered — not electricity

Looked framework-sized (the audit’s biggest braids), but fail the bar — recorded so they don’t get re-proposed.

CandidateVerdictWhy
defineMutation (optimisticOverlay)helperOne module, one domain (canvas layouts); the write echo is already owned by surface’s metadata subscription. Pending is a UI cache, not a transport axis.
@kolu/fs-watchhelperOwns a refcount/debounce lifecycle, not a streaming transport — no wire contract, Node-bound. A @kolu/shared helper, not a socket.
@kolu/geometrydomain-coupledTileLayout binds it to a server-persisted domain type; a Rect package re-adapts at every boundary — moves the braid, doesn’t dissolve it.
@kolu/surface-publishalready-coveredSurface’s cellHandlers/collectionHandlers/pollOnEvent already own server snapshot+delta. Only terminal.attach is a raw escape hatch.
@kolu/commands (palette)domain-coupledPalette engine is already agnostic; all coupling is isolated in createCommands, which must stay in the app. Extracting deletes ~0 kolu lines.
God-procedure splitrefactorIts biggest dissolvable chunk is solid-xterm (above); the rest is kolu orchestration — a refactor, not a receptacle.
provider DAG / tap-stream orchestration (server/terminalBackend/providers.ts)seam-pendingThe shape (channels, hooks, record) → void that wires cwd/title/commandRun/foreground/git/PR taps into the metadata surface reads like a host-agnostic socket — but it has exactly one host today (local.ts). The second host (SSH over surface-nix-host) is the proof it needs; extract when it lands, not before. Speculative until then.
consumeStream (async-iterable consumption)seam-pendingA Layer-0 pattern-as-artifact: the for await + isExpectedCleanupError + onItem helper lives at Terminal.tsx:119 today. Other async-iterable consumers exist but are not the same shape — server/session.ts:122 is a bare for await autosave loop with no cleanup-error classifier or onItem, and the codex/opencode WAL watchers are callback/refcount based through createWalSubscription, not async iterables. So the seam is a possible helper, not yet a duplicated pattern: if a second site grows the same isExpectedCleanupError+onItem need, fold them into one @kolu/surface/solid helper. Either way it hides only a bounded cleanup/error rule — a helper, not a transport; don’t grandstand it as electricity.
stickyModifiers / soft-keyboard inputleaf~20-line character/attribute transforms (mobile modifier encoding; iOS contenteditable shim). Bounded algorithms, xterm-shadow-DOM-specific. nonempty-tier leaves, not receptacles.
useThemeManagerdomain-coupledWraps useTerminalStore() and mutates client.terminal.setTheme(...) — terminal-specific. The theme catalog is already its own leaf (terminal-themes); the manager is app glue.
drag/resize tiling (@thisbeyond/solid-dnd + resizeGeometry)domain-coupledThe drag library is already external; the in-app resizeGeometry is canvas/tile-rect math bound to a server-persisted layout type — extracting a Rect package re-adapts at every boundary (the @kolu/geometry finding above).
notification / badge channel (useTerminalAlerts + useActivityAlerts)seam-pendingThe volatility is real and hard — cross-engine badge (navigator.setAppBadge) + the SW-only ServiceWorkerRegistration.showNotification() path (the page-level new Notification() is illegal in installed PWAs) + permission/legacy-worker-takeover quirks. But it is sprayed: the badge setter lives in useTerminalAlerts, the SW banner in useActivityAlerts, each owning its own “when to alert” terminal-domain policy. A Layer-0 missing seam — the channel wants one owner (a @kolu/solid-notify carrying badge + SW-notification + permission, leaving policy to the consumer), but until a second consumer needs it that’s a refactor, not a proven receptacle.
service worker / offline cachealready-coveredregisterServiceWorker / retireServiceWorker + the no-store-shell / immutable-hashed-asset freshness contract are owned by @kolu/surface-app (done, drishti proof). SW lifecycle is a detail inside the app-shell, not a stand-alone transport.
clipboard drag-drop upload (Terminal.tsx)domain-coupledThe HTML drag API is generic, but the handler binds file → client.terminal.uploadFile() → server clipboard-dir → bracketed-paste path. The transport is one-shot RPC, the validation terminal-specific. Inline app behavior, not a receptacle.
multi-tab coordinationnot a kolu concernNo BroadcastChannel / SharedWorker / storage-event sync exists; kolu is one session per window. persistedPref is a validate-on-read leaf, not cross-tab coordination. A real transport if kolu ever syncs across tabs — not a gap today.
visibility / wake-lockleaf / no volatility yetThe Page Visibility API is used today as a few bounded visibilitychange listeners — clear the badge when refocused (useTerminalAlerts), gate alert banners (useActivityAlerts), and a defensive refit-on-tab-visible (refitOnTabVisible.ts / Dock.tsx). Each is a leaf-sized rule, not a hard cross-engine volatility behind a socket. No wakeLock usage exists at all — there is nothing to encapsulate. If a wake-lock-during-active-PTY need ever lands, the screen-staying-awake lifecycle would belong with the xterm/terminal viewport (the forthcoming solid-xterm), not its own receptacle. A non-find, recorded so it isn’t re-proposed as a transport.

Bar from Juval Löwy, Righting Software (receptacle/volatility) + Rich Hickey, Simple Made Easy (complecting); one-socket-not-a-topic-bundle is .agency/lowy.md §6.5. The framework reduces to four network transports (state / process / agent-identity, plus the host/link substrate they ride for the remote case) and a fifth — @kolu/sandbox-bridge — across the parent↔opaque-origin-frame gap, plus a ring of UI-adapter receptacles (and a clipboard candidate still in client/src awaiting its @kolu/* extraction) and leaves; the leaf-vs-receptacle line is held by test ② (hard volatility) honestly applied — url-shape, solid-fileview, and createWalSubscription are extracted packages that are still leaves. file:line verified against the codebase; ③ demonstrated by drishti — a foreign consumer (kolu is the first) that vendors @kolu/surface via npins. anyagent’s three independent agent integrations prove ② (one unchanged contract absorbs the per-agent volatility) but not yet ③: they’re in-tree kolu packages, not a foreign app on a published @kolu/*.

← the Atlas github ↗
